title: A Day as a Field Robotics Engineer
setting: Woodside Energy autonomous inspection programme, Karratha WA (LNG processing facility)
companySize: Large energy company (3,500+ employees, 15-person robotics and autonomous systems team)
description: James O'Brien is a Field Robotics Engineer at Woodside Energy, responsible for deploying and maintaining autonomous inspection robots at one of Australia's largest LNG processing plants. His robots — a fleet of Boston Dynamics Spot quadrupeds and custom-built drone systems — perform routine inspection of pipework, pressure vessels, and flare stacks that would otherwise require human workers to access dangerous or hard-to-reach areas. James splits his time between the Perth head office (programming, simulation, analysis) and fly-in-fly-out (FIFO) rotations at the Karratha plant (deployment, troubleshooting, operator training).
salary: $140,000 - $190,000 AUD per year (includes FIFO allowances, on-site bonuses, and superannuation)

schedule.activity: Wake up at the FIFO camp, breakfast, shuttle to the plant
schedule.note: Two-week on, one-week off rotation. The camp is comfortable but remote — 1,500 km from Perth.

tools: PPE: hard hat, safety glasses, steel caps, FR clothing, gas monitor
schedule.note: Every task on an operational LNG plant requires a safety permit. Today's work: deploying Spot for a pipe rack inspection and a drone for a flare stack survey.
Check battery charge (both Spot batteries and payload battery), verify LiDAR and thermal camera payloads are functioning, load today's mission file

schedule.activity: Deploy Spot for autonomous pipe rack inspection
schedule.note: Spot walks a pre-programmed route through a pipe rack, capturing thermal images of flanges (looking for leaks) and acoustic readings of valves (detecting abnormal vibration). James monitors from a safe distance.

schedule.activity: Download and review Spot inspection data
schedule.note: Review thermal images for hot spots indicating potential flange leaks. Flag one anomaly on a valve — elevated temperature compared to baseline. Send to the maintenance team for investigation.

schedule.activity: Pre-flight checks and CASA compliance for drone inspection
schedule.note: Preparing for a flare stack inspection at 80 metres height — checking weather (wind under 20 km/h), confirming airspace clearance with the site controller, completing pre-flight checklist

schedule.activity: Fly drone inspection of flare stack
schedule.note: Flying a pre-programmed waypoint mission around the flare stack, capturing high-resolution visual and thermal imagery of the structure, tips, and refractory lining from every angle

schedule.activity: Process drone data and generate inspection report
schedule.note: Stitch images into a 3D model of the flare stack, annotate defects visible in the imagery, generate a report for the integrity engineering team

schedule.activity: Lunch at the plant mess hall
schedule.note: Chat with integrity engineers about the morning's findings — the thermal anomaly James flagged on the valve aligns with vibration data they collected last month
Spot has been struggling with a section of the route near steam vents — the steam confuses the depth cameras. Implementing a waypoint adjustment and adding a steam avoidance behaviour.
Teaching two plant operators how to deploy Spot for routine inspections without engineering support — the goal is to make robot inspection a standard operating procedure, not a special event

schedule.activity: Engineering meeting with Perth head office (video call)
schedule.note: Weekly sync with the Perth robotics team — discussing fleet management software development, next quarter's deployment targets, and a new underwater inspection robot pilot

schedule.activity: End of shift: charge robots, update logs, secure equipment
schedule.note: Ensure all robots are charging, all data is backed up, all permits are closed, and tomorrow's mission files are prepared

schedule.activity: Shuttle back to camp, gym, dinner, downtime
schedule.note: FIFO life: structured routine, good facilities, but you miss home. Video call with family after dinner. Read up on the latest Spot SDK release notes before bed.

responsibilities: Deploy and operate autonomous inspection robots (ground and aerial) at operational energy facilities
Develop and maintain mission programs for routine and ad-hoc inspections
Process and analyse inspection data (thermal, visual, acoustic, 3D models) to identify defects and anomalies
Ensure all robotic operations comply with site safety procedures and CASA regulations (for drones)
Train plant operators and maintenance staff to use robotic inspection tools
Troubleshoot robot hardware and software issues in the field
Contribute to the development of the company's autonomous inspection strategy
Collaborate with integrity engineers, maintenance teams, and operations to integrate robotic inspection into standard workflows
challenges: Operating robots in extreme conditions — 45+ degree heat, dust, humidity, corrosive salt air near the coast
Working within strict safety procedures that add time to every task (permit-to-work, gas testing, confined space protocols)
FIFO lifestyle — two weeks away from home, limited social life on site, potential impact on relationships and mental health
Keeping robots reliable in harsh environments — dust ingress, thermal stress, and vibration damage components
Convincing traditional operations teams that robots are a complement to, not a replacement for, human inspectors
Limited internet bandwidth on remote sites — difficult to download software updates or access cloud resources
rewards: Directly improving safety — every inspection done by a robot is one less human climbing scaffolding or entering a confined space
Working with cutting-edge technology (Spot, custom drones, AI-powered defect detection) in a real industrial setting
Excellent salary and benefits — FIFO roles in the Australian energy sector are well-compensated
The challenge and satisfaction of making robots work reliably in conditions they were not designed for
Contributing to Australia's energy sector while driving it toward more sustainable and safer operations
Building rare expertise at the intersection of robotics and energy — a growing field with few experienced practitioners
careerPath: Junior Field Robotics Engineer → Senior Field Robotics Engineer → Robotics Team Lead → Manager of Autonomous Systems
Alternative: move to a robotics company (Boston Dynamics, Emesent, Flyability) as an applications engineer
Alternative: transition to a consulting role, helping multiple energy and mining companies deploy robotic inspection programs
Alternative: specialise in a niche (underwater inspection robots, AI-based defect detection) and build a deep technical reputation
